summary: Fowler explores motion graphics tools for creating animated diagrams in presentations. He evaluates Adobe After Effects and Apple Motion (both expensive at ~$1000), with After Effects offering a trial version while Apple frustratingly doesn't. He experimented with Blender for 3D animation but found the cognitive overhead of 3D thinking and Blender's idiosyncratic UI made it slower than After Effects. His main frustration is that all these tools are overloaded with features he doesn't need, making them harder to use for his specific purpose of animating presentation diagrams.
key_points: Professional motion graphics tools (After Effects, Motion) are expensive at ~$1000 and may be overkill for presentation diagrams
Apple's lack of trial versions for software is a frustrating barrier to evaluation
3D animation with Blender proved slower due to cognitive overhead and poor UI design
Feature-bloated tools create friction when you only need a subset of their capabilities
money_quotes: Infuriatingly I can't do the same with Motion since Apple don't provide trial versions of their software.
Thinking in 3D is more difficult, and I'm not sure the results are usually worth the extra effort involved.
The UI for blender is very idiosyncratic, which made it frustratingly difficult to do even simple things.
All the tools I've been looking at so far do lots of things that I'm not so interested in, which makes it harder to use them for my main purpose.
key_insight: Tools designed to do everything often become harder to use for any specific purpose, creating friction for users with focused needs.
